shower.thought: We spend the first part of our lives learning how to be alive, and the second part forgetting how to do it.
The only time you see your face is when you're looking at a reflection or a photo—never the way everyone else sees it.
You've never actually seen the color you're looking at; you've only seen your brain's interpretation of it.
Silence isn't the absence of sound—it's just sounds too quiet for you to hear, which means the world is never actually quiet.
If you're waiting for the right moment to do something, you're always waiting for a moment that doesn't exist yet.
The worst part about having good ideas in the shower is that your brain knows the shower is coming and floods you with them on purpose.
Every person you meet has already lived an entire life before they met you, and you're only seeing one angle of them.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you're technically living with a different person than who you were yesterday.
The only proof that time is passing is that things keep changing, but change is the only way we know time exists.
Somewhere right now, someone is having the worst day of their life while someone else is having the best, and they have no idea the other person exists.
You're never actually looking at the present moment—by the time your brain processes what you see, it's already happened.
The word "goodbye" is the only word we use that we never want to say.
Most of what you know about yourself comes from what other people have told you that you are.
If aliens ever found us, the first question they'd probably ask is why we spend so much time staring at glowing rectangles.
Your body is the only house you can never leave, so maybe you should stop treating it like you're just renting it.
We're all just one bad decision away from a completely different life, and we make those decisions without knowing which ones they are.
The scariest thing about death isn't the dying—it's that it's the one thing you can't prepare for because you'll never get feedback.
You probably have a life-changing realization at least once a year that you'll completely forget by next year.
If you stopped learning right now, you'd spend the rest of your life slowly becoming dumber.
The people who annoy you most are probably just reflecting back the parts of yourself you don't want to see.

motivational.thought:
Your potential is not something you find—it's something you build, one choice at a time.
— Unknown

The difference between who you are and who you want to be is what you do.
— Unknown

Comfort is the enemy of growth, but discomfort is the classroom.
— Unknown

You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems.
— Unknown

Regret for things we did can be tempered by time; it is regret for the things we did not do that is inconsolable.
— Sydney Smith

The person you become is determined less by the cards you're dealt than by how you decide to play them.
— Unknown

Momentum is built in the margins—in the small decisions nobody's watching.
— Unknown

Your network determines your net worth, but your mindset determines your network.
— Unknown

Comparison is the thief of progress; comparison is also the thief of joy.
— Unknown

Excellence is not a skill, it's a standard.
— Unknown

You attract what you are, not what you want.
— Unknown

The only thing standing between you and your goals is the story you keep telling yourself about why you can't reach them.
— Unknown

Progress whispers; only regret screams.
— Unknown

What lies behind us and what lies ahead of us are tiny matters compared to what lies within us.
— Ralph Waldo Emerson

Discipline is remembering what you want when you no longer want it.
— Unknown

The gap between your current self and your future self is not time—it's decisions.
— Unknown

Success is the sum of small efforts repeated day in and day out.
— Robert Collier

You cannot control the outcome, but you can control the effort. Master the effort.
— Unknown

Wisdom is knowing what to ignore.
— Unknown

The cost of your dreams is paid in advance, in the dark, when nobody is watching.
— Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
My therapist asked why I keep sabotaging my relationships.
I said, "I don't know—ask my last three ex-partners. Oh wait, you can't."

I told my friend he was drinking too much.
He said, "I'm not drinking too much, I'm drinking the perfect amount—just not fast enough."

The worst part about having insomnia is you're always tired.
The best part is you get to experience tomorrow's regrets today.

My dad said, "Son, I'm proud of you."
Then he woke up.

I started taking antidepressants.
Now I'm a sad person who's chemically prevented from doing anything about it.

My wife asked if I was listening to her.
I said yes, but honestly I was just looking at her mouth and nodding.
She left me.
Best decision she ever made.

I'm terrible at math, so I became a fortune teller.
I tell people their future and they're never right, but I'm never wrong.

My doctor said I have six months to live.
I said I can't afford the bill.
He said, "Take a year."

I asked my boss for a raise.
He said, "You're doing great work, but the company's going through a difficult time."
Two months later, he bought a boat.
I burned it down.
I didn't get the raise.

People say money can't buy happiness.
But it can buy a jet ski.
You ever seen someone sad on a jet ski?
That's because they drowned.

My girlfriend said she needed space.
So I moved to another continent.
She said that wasn't what she meant.
I said she was being unclear.

I went to a grief counselor after my dog died.
She asked, "How long have you been feeling this way?"
I said, "Well, he's been dead for three days, but I've hated him for seven years."
She stopped taking notes.

My therapist asked what my biggest fear is.
I said rejection.
She said, "That's very common."
I said, "Does that mean you reject me too?"
She asked me to leave.

I love my job.
The commute, the coworkers, the pay—I love telling people I love my job while slowly dying inside.

My friend said he was going to kill himself.
I said, "Don't be dramatic."
Turns out he was being literal.
I felt like an idiot at the funeral.

I started a support group for people with commitment issues.
Nobody showed up to the first meeting.
They all cancelled the night before.
I'm actually not surprised.

My wife said I never remember our anniversary.
I said, "That's not true—I remember last year when you got mad."

I told my kid he could be anything he wanted when he grew up.
He's now a professional disappointment.
I guess I was right.

My therapist said I'm emotionally unavailable.
I didn't respond for three weeks.
She charged me for the time anyway.

I went to a motivational speaker.
He said, "You can do anything you put your mind to."
I put my mind to forgetting that speech.
It worked.
